did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used computer vision and machine learning techniques for skin disease characterization and melanoma detection. Specifically, it extracted statistical textural features (mean, variance, energy of co-occurrence matrix) from color texture images using the ISIC2017 and SD-198 datasets. It employed a K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) classifier with K=3 to classify skin diseases (melanoma vs. non-melanoma/benign lesions), achieving an accuracy of 85.1% on the ISIC2017 dataset using highly ranked features.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the selection of statistical textural features for skin disease characterization. It describes specific methods (co-occurrence matrix, KNN classifier), datasets used (SD-198, ISIC2017), and reports new quantitative results (accuracy metrics) rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Selection of statistic textural features for skin disease characterization toward melanoma detection
journal: 2022 8th International Conference on Control, Decision and Information Technologies (CoDIT)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of early melanoma detection by developing an automated system that characterizes skin lesions using statistical textural features from color texture images. The researchers utilized the SD-198 and ISIC2017 datasets to extract features such as mean, variance, and energy from co-occurrence matrices, evaluating their degree of characterization within the lesion and surrounding healthy skin. A K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) classifier with K=3 was employed to distinguish between melanoma and non-melanoma (benign) lesions. The evaluation demonstrated that using highly ranked features derived from the whole image significantly improved classification performance compared to using segmented lesions or poorly ranked features. The method achieved a maximum accuracy of 85.1% on the ISIC2017 dataset, highlighting the importance of integrating healthy skin texture data for accurate diagnosis.

all_task_types.target: Skin disease categories (melanoma vs. non-melanoma/benign lesions; or five specific classes: basal cell carcinoma, blue nevus, malignant melanoma, nevus incipiens, seborrheic keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) on the ISIC2017 dataset and multiclass classification (five specific skin diseases) on the SD-198 dataset using a K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) classifier with K=3. The goal is to assign discrete diagnostic labels to skin lesions based on statistical textural features.

datasets_used.name: SD-198
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing 198 skin diseases represented by 6,584 images, used to evaluate feature characterization for five specific skin disease classes.

use.training: Used for feature selection and classification evaluation; split into 40 images for training and 20 images for testing per class.
use.testing: Used for testing classification performance with KNN classifier.
use.other: Used to calculate the degree of characterization (J) for statistical textural features across Red, Green, and Blue color components.

datasets_used.name: ISIC2017
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2017 dataset, containing three kinds of skin diseases, used for melanoma vs. non-melanoma classification.

use.testing: Used for binary classification of melanoma vs. non-melanoma (formed by gathering seborrheic keratosis and benign nevus images) using highly ranked features.
use.other: Ground truth segmentation maps were used to segment the Region of Interest (lesion) for comparison with whole-image texture analysis.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the discriminative power and effectiveness of statistical textural features for skin disease classification.
To classify skin diseases based on the extracted statistical textural features.
To classify skin diseases (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) using highly ranked features.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It utilized a 'degree of characterization' metric (J) for feature selection and reported classification accuracy as the primary performance metric.

SD-198.experiment: Feature selection and classification of five skin disease classes (basal cell carcinoma, blue nevus, malignant melanoma, nevus incipiens, seborrheic keratosis)

dataset_details.split: 40 images for training, 20 images for testing per class

SD-198.notes: Using highly ranked features ensures the best performance in classifying the five skin diseases compared to using all features or poorly ranked features. The degree of characterization J was higher when using the whole image compared to segmented lesions.

ISIC2017.experiment: Binary classification of melanoma vs. non-melanoma (benign lesions)

Whole_image_vs_Segmented_lesion.observation: Degrees of characterization of each statistic textural feature are higher when using the whole image compared to the segmented lesion.

ISIC2017.notes: Using attributes having the highest degrees of characterization increasingly improves the diagnosis performance. Using segmented lesions achieves an accuracy of 59.3%, which is around 25% decrease against using the whole image. Integrating healthy skin texture data is complementary to characterizing textures of different skin conditions.

architecture_details.feature_extraction_method: Statistical textural features extracted from color texture images (Red, Green, Blue components) using co-occurrence matrices and histogram analysis
feature_sets: Highly ranked (Set1): Mean, Variance, Energy of co-occurrence matrix
Moderate ranked (Set2): Moment of order 3 and 4, Mean of histogram, Uniformity
Poorly ranked (Set3): Rest of the textural attributes

dataset_splits.SD-198: 40 images for training, 20 images for testing per class

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Binary classification (Melanoma vs Non-melanoma) or Multiclass classification (5 skin diseases)
classification_or_output_head.decision_rule: KNN voting or Minimum Euclidean Distance

preprocessing_techniques: Segmentation of the Region of Interest (lesion) using ground truth binary masks provided in the ISIC2017 dataset to isolate the lesion from the background.
Extraction of Red, Green, and Blue color component matrices from the color texture images.
conclusions: Using highly ranked statistical textural features (mean, variance, and energy of the co-occurrence matrix) ensures the best performance in classifying five specific skin diseases compared to using all features or poorly ranked features.
The degree of characterization for statistical textural features is higher when using the whole image (including surrounding healthy skin) compared to using only the segmented lesion.
Integrating healthy skin texture data is complementary to characterizing pathological skin conditions and improves the diagnosis process.
Using attributes with the highest degrees of characterization increasingly improves the diagnosis performance for melanoma detection.
The proposed method achieved a maximum accuracy of 85.1% on the ISIC2017 dataset for distinguishing melanoma from non-melanoma lesions using highly ranked features on the whole image.
Segmenting only the lesion results in significantly lower classification accuracy (59.3%) compared to using the whole image (85.1%), representing a decrease of approximately 25%.
The experiments demonstrate promising results in recognizing melanoma by focusing on texture manifestations, despite characterizing only a limited number of dermatologist criteria.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small sample size for the SD-198 dataset (only 60 images per class, with 40 used for training and 20 for testing), which limits the statistical power and generalizability of the multiclass classification results.

The SD-198 multiclass classification accuracy is extremely low (24% for highly ranked features), suggesting the method struggles to distinguish between the five specific benign/malignant classes despite the theoretical feature selection.

The ISIC2017 experiment uses a non-standard binary grouping for the 'non-melanoma' class, combining seborrheic keratosis and benign nevus, which may not reflect clinical reality where these have distinct management pathways.

The paper does not report standard deviation, confidence intervals, or statistical significance tests (p-values) for the reported accuracy metrics (85.1% and 59.3%), making it difficult to assess the robustness of the results.

The feature selection method ('degree of characterization J') is calculated on the entire dataset rather than using a hold-out validation set, posing a risk of data leakage and overfitting during the feature selection process.
The study lacks external validation on independent datasets beyond SD-198 and ISIC2017, limiting the assessment of clinical relevance and generalizability to diverse skin types or imaging devices.

The comparison between whole-image and segmented lesion analysis relies on ground truth segmentation maps provided in ISIC2017; however, the paper does not evaluate the impact of segmentation errors or inter-observer variability on the texture feature extraction.

The KNN classifier (K=3) is a simple, non-parametric model that does not capture complex non-linear relationships in texture data, potentially underperforming compared to modern deep learning approaches without extensive hyperparameter tuning or ensemble methods.
